title: Limits at Infinity and Horizontal Asymptotes

epigraph.text: He has made everything beautiful in its time. He has also set eternity in the human heart; yet no one can fathom what God has done from beginning to end.
epigraph.quote: The infinite! No other question has ever moved so profoundly the spirit of man.

content.text: Looking Far Away

In Section 2.4, we studied what happens when a function's *output* blows
up—the values escape to infinity near a finite trouble spot. Now we ask
the complementary question: what happens to the output when the *input*
goes to infinity? If we travel far, far along the $x$-axis, does the function
eventually settle down?

Let us be perfectly clear about the distinction, because students often
confuse these two ideas:

content.title: The Two Infinities --- Do Not Confuse Them!
content.content:
**Infinite limits** (Section 2.4):

$x \to a$ (input approaches a finite number)
$f(x) \to \pm\infty$ (output blows up)
Graphical signature: **vertical asymptote** at $x = a$.

**Limits at infinity** (this section):

$x \to \pm\infty$ (input goes to infinity)
$f(x) \to L$ (output settles down to a finite number)
Graphical signature: **horizontal asymptote** at $y = L$.

**Memory aid:** "Infinite limit" $=$ *infinite output*.
"Limit at infinity" $=$ *at infinite input*.
The word *at* tells you which quantity is going to infinity.

content.text:
Consider the function $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$. We already know (from
Section 2.4) that $f$ has a vertical asymptote at $x = 0$. But what
happens on the other end—as $x$ grows very large?

As $x$ grows without bound in either direction, $f(x)$ approaches $0$. The
function settles down; the line $y = 0$ acts like a "landing strip" that
the graph approaches but never quite reaches. We write:
\[
 \limto{x \to \infty} \frac{1}{x} = 0,
 \qquad
 \limto{x \to -\infty} \frac{1}{x} = 0.
\]
Formal Definitions

content.title: Limit at Infinity
content.content:
Let $f$ be a function defined on some interval $(a, \infty)$. We write
\[
 \limto{x \to \infty} f(x) = L
\]
if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists a number $N$ such that
\[
 x > N \implies \abs{f(x) - L} < \varepsilon.
\]
In words: we can make $f(x)$ as close to $L$ as we wish by taking $x$
sufficiently large.

Similarly, $\limto{x \to -\infty} f(x) = L$ means that for every
$\varepsilon > 0$ there exists a number $N$ such that
$x < N \implies \abs{f(x) - L} < \varepsilon$.

content.title: Horizontal Asymptote
content.content:
The line $y = L$ is a **horizontal asymptote** of the graph of $f$ if
\[
 \limto{x \to \infty} f(x) = L
 \qquad\text{or}\qquad
 \limto{x \to -\infty} f(x) = L
 \qquad\text{(or both).}
\]

content.text: Note two important facts:

items:
A function can have *at most two* horizontal asymptotes—one as
 $x \to \infty$ and one as $x \to -\infty$. (It could have zero, one,
 or two.) This contrasts with vertical asymptotes, of which a function
 may have infinitely many (consider $\tan x$).

A function *can* cross a horizontal asymptote. The HA describes
 *end behavior*—what happens far out along the $x$-axis. Near
 the origin, the function may cross the line $y = L$ freely.

content.text: The Fundamental Building Block

The following result is the key to evaluating almost every limit at infinity
for rational functions:

content.title: Limits of $1/x^n$ at Infinity
content.content:
For any positive integer $n$,
\[
 \limto{x \to \infty} \frac{1}{x^n} = 0
 \qquad\text{and}\qquad
 \limto{x \to -\infty} \frac{1}{x^n} = 0.
\]
More generally, for any positive real number $r$,
$\limto{x \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{x^r} = 0$.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This theorem, combined with limit laws (which apply to limits at infinity
just as they do to ordinary limits), is the basis of the master technique
for rational functions.
The Master Technique: Divide by the Highest Power

content.title: Limits at Infinity for Rational Functions
content.content:
To evaluate $\limto{x \to \infty} \dfrac{p(x)}{q(x)}$ where $p$ and $q$ are
polynomials, **divide every term in the numerator and denominator by
the highest power of $x$ appearing in the denominator.** Then apply
Theorem [thm:one-over-xn]: every term with $x$ in the denominator
vanishes, and you are left with the answer.

content.text:
This technique yields a clean rule that depends only on comparing the
*degrees* of the numerator and denominator:

content.title: Horizontal Asymptotes of Rational Functions
content.content:
Let $f(x) = \dfrac{a_n x^n + \cdots + a_0}{b_m x^m + \cdots + b_0}$ with
$a_n \ne 0$ and $b_m \ne 0$.

1. If $n < m$ (degree of numerator $<$ degree of denominator), then
 \[
 \limto{x \to \pm\infty} f(x) = 0.
 \]
 Horizontal asymptote: $y = 0$.

2. If $n = m$ (degrees equal), then
 \[
 \limto{x \to \pm\infty} f(x) = \frac{a_n}{b_m}.
 \]
 Horizontal asymptote: $y = a_n / b_m$ (ratio of leading coefficients).

3. If $n > m$ (degree of numerator $>$ degree of denominator), then
 \[
 \limto{x \to \pm\infty} f(x) = \pm\infty.
 \]
 No horizontal asymptote. The function grows without bound.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: The Rule Is a Shortcut, Not a Replacement
content.content:
The degree-comparison rule in Theorem [thm:rational-ha] is a wonderful
shortcut, but you should understand *why* it works (the divide-by-highest-power
technique) and be able to carry out the full calculation when asked.
On exams, you may be required to show the work, not just state the rule.

content.text: Worked Examples

content.title: Degree of numerator less than denominator
content.solution:
The highest power of $x$ in the denominator is $x^2$. Divide every term by $x^2$:
\[
 \frac{2x + 5}{x^2 - 3}
 = \frac{\dfrac{2x}{x^2} + \dfrac{5}{x^2}}{\dfrac{x^2}{x^2} - \dfrac{3}{x^2}}
 = \frac{\dfrac{2}{x} + \dfrac{5}{x^2}}{1 - \dfrac{3}{x^2}}.
\]
As $x \to \infty$, each term with $x$ in the denominator approaches $0$:
\[
 \limto{x \to \infty} \frac{\dfrac{2}{x} + \dfrac{5}{x^2}}{1 - \dfrac{3}{x^2}}
 = \frac{0 + 0}{1 - 0} = 0.
\]
This confirms case (i) of Theorem [thm:rational-ha]: degree $1 <$ degree $2$,
so the horizontal asymptote is $y = 0$.

content.title: Equal degrees --- the most common case
content.solution:
The highest power of $x$ in the denominator is $x^2$. Divide every term by $x^2$:
\[
 \frac{3x^2 + 1}{5x^2 - 2}
 = \frac{3 + \dfrac{1}{x^2}}{5 - \dfrac{2}{x^2}}.
\]
As $x \to \infty$:
\[
 \limto{x \to \infty} \frac{3 + \dfrac{1}{x^2}}{5 - \dfrac{2}{x^2}}
 = \frac{3 + 0}{5 - 0} = \frac{3}{5}.
\]
The horizontal asymptote is $y = 3/5$. Note that this is simply the ratio
of the leading coefficients, exactly as Theorem [thm:rational-ha](ii)
predicts.

The same computation works for $x \to -\infty$, since $1/x^2 \to 0$ regardless
of the sign of $x$. So the horizontal asymptote is the same on both sides.

content.title: Degree of numerator greater than denominator
content.solution:
Divide every term by $x^2$ (the highest power in the denominator):
\[
 \frac{x^3 - 2x}{4x^2 + 1}
 = \frac{x - \dfrac{2}{x}}{4 + \dfrac{1}{x^2}}.
\]
As $x \to \infty$, the numerator $\to \infty$ while the denominator $\to 4$.
Therefore:
\[
 \limto{x \to \infty} \frac{x^3 - 2x}{4x^2 + 1} = \infty.
\]
There is no horizontal asymptote. The function grows without bound as
$x \to \infty$.

content.title: Both vertical and horizontal asymptotes
content.problem:
Find *all* asymptotes of $f(x) = \dfrac{2x - 1}{x + 3}$ and evaluate
the relevant limits.
content.solution:
**Horizontal asymptote:** The numerator and denominator both have
degree $1$. By Theorem [thm:rational-ha](ii):
\[
 \limto{x \to \infty} \frac{2x - 1}{x + 3} = \frac{2}{1} = 2,
 \qquad
 \limto{x \to -\infty} \frac{2x - 1}{x + 3} = 2.
\]
Horizontal asymptote: $y = 2$.

**Vertical asymptote:** The denominator is zero at $x = -3$, and the
numerator $2(-3) - 1 = -7 \ne 0$, so $x = -3$ is a vertical asymptote.

- As $x \to -3^+$: $(x + 3) \to 0^+$ and numerator $\to -7$.
 So $f(x) \to \dfrac{-7}{0^+} = -\infty$.

- As $x \to -3^-$: $(x + 3) \to 0^-$ and numerator $\to -7$.
 So $f(x) \to \dfrac{-7}{0^-} = +\infty$.

This function has one vertical asymptote ($x = -3$) and one horizontal
asymptote ($y = 2$). The graph approaches the horizontal line $y = 2$ far
to the left and far to the right, while blowing up near $x = -3$.

content.title: Pierre de Fermat (1601--1665)
content.content:
Long before the formal development of limits, Pierre de Fermat—a French
lawyer and amateur mathematician—developed methods for finding tangent lines
and maxima/minima that implicitly used limiting processes. Fermat also studied
the curve $y = 1/x$ (the "hyperbola of Fermat") and investigated the area
under it, discovering what we now recognize as the connection between the
hyperbola and the natural logarithm. His work on the long-run behavior of
curves anticipated the modern study of asymptotes. Fermat was a devout Catholic
who saw mathematics as a noble pursuit of truth, famously scribbling his most
tantalizing conjecture—Fermat's Last Theorem—in the margin of his copy of
Diophantus.

content.title: A limit at infinity involving a square root
content.solution:
This is an "$\infty - \infty$" indeterminate form. As $x \to \infty$,
both $\sqrt{x^2 + 4x}$ and $x$ go to infinity, so we cannot simply subtract.

The technique is to **rationalize**—multiply and divide by the conjugate:
\[
 \sqrt{x^2 + 4x} - x
 = \frac{\bigl(\sqrt{x^2 + 4x} - x\bigr)\bigl(\sqrt{x^2 + 4x} + x\bigr)}
 {\sqrt{x^2 + 4x} + x}
 = \frac{(x^2 + 4x) - x^2}{\sqrt{x^2 + 4x} + x}
 = \frac{4x}{\sqrt{x^2 + 4x} + x}.
\]
Now divide numerator and denominator by $x$ (which is positive for $x \to \infty$):
\[
 = \frac{4}{\dfrac{\sqrt{x^2 + 4x}}{x} + 1}
 = \frac{4}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{4}{x}} + 1}.
\]
As $x \to \infty$, $\dfrac{4}{x} \to 0$, so:
\[
 \limto{x \to \infty} \bigl(\sqrt{x^2 + 4x} - x\bigr)
 = \frac{4}{\sqrt{1 + 0} + 1} = \frac{4}{2} = 2.
\]

content.title: Exponential decay
content.solution:
Recall that $e^{-x} = \dfrac{1}{e^x}$. As $x \to \infty$, $e^x$ grows
without bound, so $e^{-x} \to 0$. Therefore:
\[
 \limto{x \to \infty} (3 + 5e^{-x}) = 3 + 5(0) = 3.
\]
The line $y = 3$ is a horizontal asymptote. Physically, if this function
models a cooling process, the object approaches a steady-state temperature
of $3$ (in whatever units).

What about $x \to -\infty$? As $x \to -\infty$, $e^{-x} = e^{|x|} \to \infty$,
so $3 + 5e^{-x} \to \infty$. There is no horizontal asymptote on the left—the
function grows without bound. This function has *different* behavior at
$+\infty$ and $-\infty$, which is why we must check both directions.

content.title: A slant (oblique) asymptote
content.solution:
**Vertical asymptote:** The denominator is zero at $x = 1$, and
$p(1) = 1 + 2 + 1 = 4 \ne 0$, so $x = 1$ is a vertical asymptote.

**Horizontal asymptote:** The degree of the numerator ($2$) is
greater than the degree of the denominator ($1$), so by
Theorem [thm:rational-ha](iii), there is *no* horizontal asymptote.

But something more interesting is happening. When the degree of the numerator
exceeds the degree of the denominator by *exactly one*, the graph
approaches a *slant* (oblique) asymptote—a line $y = mx + b$.

To find it, perform polynomial long division:
\[
 \frac{x^2 + 2x + 1}{x - 1} = x + 3 + \frac{4}{x - 1}.
\]
As $x \to \pm\infty$, the remainder term $\dfrac{4}{x - 1} \to 0$, so
\[
 f(x) \approx x + 3 \qquad \text{for large } \abs{x}.
\]
The line $y = x + 3$ is a **slant asymptote**. The graph of $f$
approaches this line as $x \to \pm\infty$, getting ever closer but never
quite coinciding.

**Summary of asymptotes:**

- Vertical asymptote: $x = 1$

- Slant asymptote: $y = x + 3$

- No horizontal asymptote

content.title: Complete asymptote analysis
content.problem:
Find all asymptotes of $f(x) = \dfrac{3x^2 - 12}{x^2 - x - 2}$ and
describe the end behavior.
content.solution:
Factor:
\[
 f(x) = \frac{3(x^2 - 4)}{(x - 2)(x + 1)}
 = \frac{3(x - 2)(x + 2)}{(x - 2)(x + 1)}.
\]
Cancel $(x - 2)$:
\[
 f(x) = \frac{3(x + 2)}{x + 1}, \qquad x \ne 2.
\]

**Hole:** At $x = 2$, the common factor was cancelled.
The $y$-value of the hole: $\dfrac{3(4)}{3} = 4$.
Hole at $(2, 4)$.

**Vertical asymptote:** At $x = -1$: numerator $= 3(-1 + 2) = 3 \ne 0$.
So $x = -1$ is a vertical asymptote.

- $x \to -1^+$: $(x + 1) \to 0^+$, so $f(x) \to 3/0^+ = +\infty$.

- $x \to -1^-$: $(x + 1) \to 0^-$, so $f(x) \to 3/0^- = -\infty$.

**Horizontal asymptote:** In the simplified form, both numerator
and denominator have degree $1$. The leading coefficients are $3$ and $1$.
\[
 \limto{x \to \pm\infty} \frac{3(x + 2)}{x + 1} = \frac{3}{1} = 3.
\]
Horizontal asymptote: $y = 3$.

**Summary:**

- Hole at $(2, 4)$

- Vertical asymptote: $x = -1$

- Horizontal asymptote: $y = 3$

content.text: The Dominance Hierarchy

When dealing with limits at infinity for non-rational functions, it helps to
know which functions "grow fastest." As $x \to \infty$:

\[
 \ln x \quad\ll\quad x^n \quad\ll\quad e^x \quad\ll\quad x^x
\]
where $\ll$ means "grows much slower than." More precisely:

content.title: Exponential Dominates Polynomial
content.content:
For any positive integer $n$,
\[
 \limto{x \to \infty} \frac{x^n}{e^x} = 0.
\]
Exponential growth eventually overwhelms any polynomial growth.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Polynomial Dominates Logarithmic
content.content:
For any positive real numbers $r$ and $s$,
\[
 \limto{x \to \infty} \frac{(\ln x)^s}{x^r} = 0.
\]
Even a small polynomial power eventually overwhelms any power of the logarithm.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
These results will be proved rigorously using L'H\^opital's Rule in
Chapter 4, but they are intuitive: the exponential function $e^x$ grows
so aggressively that no polynomial can keep up, and the logarithm $\ln x$
grows so sluggishly that any positive power of $x$ eventually dwarfs it.

content.title: John Wallis (1616--1703)
content.content:
The symbol $\infty$ for infinity was introduced by the English mathematician
John Wallis in his 1655 work *Tractatus de Sectionibus Conicis*. An
ordained priest in the Church of England and Savilian Professor of Geometry
at Oxford, Wallis also contributed the foundational ideas that led to the
calculus of Newton and Leibniz. His infinite product formula
$\dfrac{\pi}{2} = \dfrac{2}{1}\cdot\dfrac{2}{3}\cdot\dfrac{4}{3}\cdot
\dfrac{4}{5}\cdot\dfrac{6}{5}\cdot\dfrac{6}{7}\cdots$
remains one of the most beautiful results connecting infinity to the finite.
Wallis understood that the infinite could be tamed through careful reasoning—a
lesson this chapter teaches in the language of limits.

content.text: Oblique (Slant) Asymptotes

When the degree of the numerator exceeds the degree of the denominator by
exactly one, the function does not have a horizontal asymptote, but it does
approach a *line*:

content.title: Oblique (Slant) Asymptote
content.content:
The line $y = mx + b$ (with $m \ne 0$) is an **oblique asymptote**
(or **slant asymptote**) of $f$ if
\[
 \limto{x \to \infty} \bigl[f(x) - (mx + b)\bigr] = 0
 \qquad\text{or}\qquad
 \limto{x \to -\infty} \bigl[f(x) - (mx + b)\bigr] = 0.
\]

content.title: Finding Slant Asymptotes
content.content:
If $f(x) = \dfrac{p(x)}{q(x)}$ where $\deg(p) = \deg(q) + 1$, perform
polynomial long division to write
\[
 f(x) = (mx + b) + \frac{r(x)}{q(x)},
\]
where the degree of the remainder $r(x)$ is less than the degree of $q(x)$.
Then $\dfrac{r(x)}{q(x)} \to 0$ as $x \to \pm\infty$, so $y = mx + b$ is
the slant asymptote.

content.text: Putting It All Together

Recall the chapter devotional: the builders of Babel raised their tower
toward heaven, but heaven remained above—an asymptote they could
approach but never reach. In exactly this way, a horizontal asymptote
represents a boundary the function eternally approaches. The outputs
$f(x)$ draw closer and closer to $L$ as the input grows without bound,
yet there is always a gap, however small. The tower rises forever;
heaven remains above.

And so we see two faces of infinity in the calculus of limits. In
Section 2.4, the function's values *become* infinite—they escape
every bound as we approach a finite trouble spot. Here in Section 2.5,
we *travel* toward infinity along the $x$-axis and ask whether the
function settles down. Both involve the infinite, but from opposite
directions. Together, they give us a complete picture of a function's
global behavior: its explosions and its calms, its vertical walls and
its distant horizons.

marginNotes.text:
As far as the east is from the west, so far
has he removed our transgressions from us.

It is the glory of God to conceal a matter;
to search out a matter is the glory of kings.

Oh, the depth of the riches of the wisdom and
knowledge of God! How unsearchable his judgments, and his paths beyond
tracing out!

marginNotes.author: Blaise Pascal (1623--1662)
marginNotes.text:
The eternal silence of these infinite
spaces fills me with dread.

marginNotes.author: Georg Cantor (1845--1918)
marginNotes.text:
The fear of infinity is a form of
myopia that destroys the possibility of seeing the actual infinite, even though
it in its highest form has created and sustains us.
